prompt: What is the strongest near-term strategy for give creators the visibility and control they need to show up consistently — so that community becomes a place they're invested in, not just present on? Compare the most likely product, lifecycle, or messaging levers and recommend what to test first. Center the recommendation on improving active rate. Keep the target in mind: +25% creator posting frequency · +15% creator DAU/WAU ratio · +10% pageviews per community session — measured 90 days post-launch. Call out what additional signals would most improve confidence.

objectives.title: Give creators the visibility and control they need to show up consistently — so that community becomes a place they're invested in, not just present on.
objectives.target: +25% creator posting frequency · +15% creator DAU/WAU ratio · +10% pageviews per community session — measured 90 days post-launch
objectives.description: Creators are the engine of community engagement. When they're inactive, reader engagement drops, return visits decline, and pageview revenue suffers. The current platform gives creators no visibility into what's working and no way to manage their presence efficiently. Two weeks of creator silence measurably increases reader churn — and there is no recovery mechanism. Substack, Patreon, and Creator.co offer creator analytics and engagement tools as table stakes. Raptive Community currently has none.
objectives.theoryOfSuccess: If creators can see which content is working, schedule posts ahead of time, and be surfaced the right moments to engage — then posting frequency increases, reader churn falls, and pageview lift follows from sustained community activity.

Directing Research & Insights to analyze: What is the strongest near-term strategy for give creators the visibility and control they need to show up consistently — so that community becomes a place they're invested in, not just present on? Compare the most likely product, lifecycle, or messaging levers and recommend what to test first. Center the recommendation on improving active rate. Keep the target in mind: +25% creator posting frequency · +15% creator DAU/WAU ratio · +10% pageviews per community session — measured 90 days post-launch. Call out what additional signals would most improve confidence.

MODE: decision memo

READINESS: Design Strategy has framed the brief and scoped the evidence request.

WHAT WOULD IMPROVE CONFIDENCE: If the evidence is thin, Research & Insights should surface the next 1-3 signals worth gathering.
messages.nextStep: Research & Insights to pull evidence and synthesize

messages.subject: Synthesis: What is the strongest near-term strategy for give creators t
messages.assumptions: Analysis based on available observations and session data.
messages.body:
RESEARCH & INSIGHTS SYNTHESIS
Creator Visibility & Control Strategy

---

SUBJECT
Available evidence is insufficient to confidently rank feature levers; recommend immediate lightweight creator research to validate whether visibility, control, or motivation is the true posting-frequency bottleneck before prioritizing product investment.

---

DETAILS
The business hypothesis is sound and metrics are well-defined, but the research foundation is thin. We have four design sessions focused on onboarding and dashboard aesthetics—not creator behavior, lapse reasons, or feature demand. The three proposed levers (analytics visibility, scheduling, engagement prompts) are plausible but untested against actual creator friction points. Before building, we need 1–2 weeks of lightweight qualitative research with lapsing creators to validate whether the bottleneck is truly visibility/control or something else entirely (motivation, time scarcity, unclear audience expectations). The onboarding-focused sessions suggest internal focus on new-creator acquisition, not lapsing-creator recovery—a strategic mismatch if the highest-ROI segment is recovery.

---

CONFIDENCE
Low — Data is thin and misaligned with the stated research objective.

---

READINESS
Weak. The brief correctly identifies that we have business objectives, segment clarity, and outcome metrics, but the evidence base does not include creator interviews, usage pattern analysis, lapse-reason data, or competitive feature audit. The design sessions present are about onboarding experience and dashboard visual design—not about what drives creator posting consistency. To move to "Partial" readiness, we need creator-facing research (5–10 interviews with lapsing creators) and a baseline metrics pull. To move to "Sufficient" readiness, we'd also need competitive audit and creator preference validation.

---

TOP FINDINGS
Finding 1: Onboarding focus, not retention focus — All four design sessions are anchored to new-creator onboarding (landing page, wizard, video content, resource center) and dashboard aesthetics, not to lapsing-creator recovery or posting-cadence drivers. This suggests internal prioritization may be misaligned with the stated highest-ROI segment (lapsing creators).  
  Segment: lapsing-creators | Confidence: High (direct evidence from session titles and problem statements)
Finding 2: No creator input on lever preferences — The three proposed levers (analytics, scheduling, engagement prompts) are inferred from competitive parity and product intuition, not from creator interviews, surveys, or stated frustrations. We don't know which friction point creators themselves perceive as the primary blocker to consistent posting.  
  Segment: lapsing-creators, high-activity-creators | Confidence: High (absence of evidence; explicit assumption in the brief)
Finding 3: Assumption about two-week silence → churn is stated, not validated — The brief asserts "two weeks of creator silence destabilizes reader churn" and uses this as a behavioral signal, but we have no data showing: (a) how many creators hit two-week silence, (b) what reader churn looks like post-lapse, or (c) whether recovery is possible at that window.  
  Segment: lapsing-creators | Confidence: Medium (stated as behavioral signal in brief, but no supporting evidence provided)

---

TOP NEEDS
Creator lapse interviews or diary study (5–10 lapsing creators, 30–45 min each) — Validate the true bottleneck. Are creators lapsing because: (a) they don't see engagement data and can't optimize, (b) posting workflow is friction-heavy (no scheduling), (c) they don't get nudged at high-engagement moments, or (d) motivation is low or external factors dominate? This single research act will most directly inform lever prioritization. Source: Conduct within 7–10 days; target creators with >7-day silence in last 30 days.
Baseline posting-frequency distribution and variance by segment (1–2 hour data pull) — What does the baseline look like? What % of lapsing creators post <1x/week? What's the posting ceiling for high-activity creators? This validates whether +25% is realistic and whether the lever should target frequency recovery or frequency improvement. Source: Platform analytics; segment by last_post_date, posting_frequency_rolling_30d, cohort_stage.
Competitive feature audit (shallow; 2–3 hours) — Which feature is most prominent in creator UX at Substack, Patreon, and Creator.co? Which do creators cite in reviews or forums as a stay/go driver? This informs whether to lead with analytics, scheduling, or messaging. Source: Competitive teardowns, Slack communities, creator forums (Twitter, Reddit, indie hacker spaces).

---

RECOMMENDATION

Do not yet prioritize feature development. Instead, immediately conduct lightweight creator research (interviews or survey) with lapsing creators to validate which of the three levers addresses the true bottleneck.

If forced to recommend a lever to test now based on first principles and industry precedent:

Test Lever B (Scheduling + Calendar) as the first lever for the following reasons:
Lowest execution risk & clearest 90-day signal path: Scheduling is a discrete, measurable feature. "Post scheduled in advance" → "posts published on schedule" → "posting frequency increases" is a linear causal path. Analytics and engagement prompts require behavior change (creator interprets data, acts on nudge); scheduling removes friction directly.
Targets lapsing creators' likely pain point: Lapsing creators often cite time scarcity and unpredictability in external research (creator surveys, industry reports). Scheduling front-loads the time investment (plan a week's posts in one session) and removes the daily friction of "when should I post today?" This is likely to unlock posting frequency faster than expecting creators to interpret analytics.
High-activity creators already have implicit discipline: Power users are already posting consistently; they likely have mental calendars or external systems (email, Notion, personal calendar). Scheduling benefits them too (consolidates tools, reduces cognitive load) but doesn't move the primary metric as dramatically. Lapsing creators, however, may re-engage faster with a simple "queue 3 posts this week" workflow.
Secondary validation from onboarding context: The four design sessions show focus on getting new creators to "first aha moment" quickly. Scheduling is an aha moment for new creators too (they ship content faster), suggesting internal confidence in its value. Consistency between new-creator and lapsing-creator use cases is a signal.
Measurable at 30-day checkpoint: Unlike analytics (which may require behavioral interpretation cycles), scheduling has an immediate proxy: posts scheduled → posts published on time → daily/weekly frequency increases. You'll see signal at 30 days; this allows mid-course pivot if needed.

Tier the rollout by segment:
Tier 1 (Week 1-4): Lapsing creators (target +25% posting frequency; largest ROI if it works).
Tier 2 (Week 5-8): High-activity creators (measure retention/expansion; likely already have external systems; measure displacement vs. adoption).
Tier 3 (Week 9-12): New creators in onboarding (measure adoption rate and impact on time-to-first-post metric).

---

PHASE

Learning phase, verging on Scaling phase.  
Reasoning: We have a clear hypothesis and defined metrics, but no feature evidence or creator-validated bottleneck diagnosis. The scope is narrow (single feature lever, fixed measurement window, clear success criteria). This is directional proof-of-concept, not yet scaling an established behavior driver. If scheduling moves +25% posting frequency at 90 days, we scale it and layer in analytics/engagement prompts. If it doesn't, we pivot to analytics or messaging.

---

ASSUMPTIONS
Creators stop posting due to workflow/visibility friction, not motivation loss — Untested. If creators lapse due to external life events, motivation loss, or unclear audience expectations, no feature will move posting frequency significantly.
Posting frequency directly correlates with reader retention — Stated in brief. Untested edge case: if reader churn is driven by content quality (not frequency), adding scheduling won't fix retention.
+25% posting frequency is achievable in 90 days — Depends on baseline. If lapsing creators average 0.5 posts/month, +25% = 0.63 posts/month (marginal). If they average 4 posts/month, +25% = 5 posts/month (plausible). Baseline data is critical.
Lapsing creators are recoverable within 90 days — Untested. If reader expectations reset after 2 weeks of silence, recovery may require longer onboarding/re-engagement than the measurement window.
Scheduling is not already available on the platform — Inferred from "table-stakes features exist elsewhere; Raptive currently has none." If scheduling exists but is buried or poorly designed, the lever becomes UI/UX, not feature addition.
Single-lever isolation is achievable — Untested. In practice, providing scheduling + new dashboard design + onboarding nudges will confound. Recommend A/B test design that isolates scheduling feature from design/messaging changes.
Creator-teams have shared visibility needs but segmentation is necessary — Stated in brief. If teams are a small segment (<15% of creators) or have identical friction to solo creators, may not be a distinct lever; may be a secondary measure.

---

FINDINGS

Finding 1: Strategic Misalignment Between Stated Objective and Available Evidence
Pattern: The research objective is to identify which lever (analytics, scheduling, engagement prompts) most directly drives +25% creator posting frequency in lapsing creators. However, all four design sessions focus on onboarding experience for new creators and dashboard aesthetics—not on lapsing-creator recovery or posting-cadence drivers.  
Evidence: Session titles: "Community Onboarding: Where Should We Focus?" (2 sessions), "Dashboard Widget Style" (2 sessions). Problem statements cite "creators who don't follow strategic guidance," "40% of signups never complete onboarding," and "current dashboard looks dated"—none directly address why creators lapse or which feature unlocks consistent posting.  
Segment Relevance: lapsing-creators (direct), high-activity-creators (indirect; retention mechanism), regular-users (onboarding-focused sessions suggest new-creator focus, not dormant-creator reactivation).  
User Stage Relevance: at_risk_users (lapsing creators not addressed in sessions); regular_users and power_users (onboarding-focused; retention mechanisms not in scope).  
Confidence: High — Direct observation of session content vs. stated objective.  
Implication: Before executing a feature lever test, internal alignment is needed on whether the priority is new-creator onboarding success (which the sessions suggest) or lapsing-creator recovery (which the brief states). If both are priorities, the team is context-switching; recommend explicit sequencing.

---

Finding 2: Proposed Feature Levers Are Inferred, Not Validated
Pattern: The brief proposes three levers (analytics visibility, scheduling, engagement prompts) as the primary vehicles for increasing posting frequency. However, there is no evidence from creator interviews, surveys, or usage patterns that these are the actual friction points creators face when deciding to lapse or resume posting.  
Evidence: The brief explicitly states under "WHAT WE LACK: Creator-stated reasons for lapses (time, discovery, motivation, workflow friction)." No interviews, surveys, or creator feedback are in the provided evidence set. The levers are inferred from competitive parity ("Substack, Patreon, Creator.co feature parity expectations") and product intuition, not from creator-validated pain.  
Segment Relevance: lapsing-creators (direct; we don't know why they specifically lapse), high-activity-creators (secondary; what keeps them consistent?), creator-teams (tertiary; do they have shared friction?).  
User Stage Relevance: at_risk_users (lapsing creators; highest-impact segment, lowest visibility into their needs).  
Confidence: High — Explicit gap acknowledged in brief; no countervailing evidence provided.  
Implication: Before committing to 8–12 weeks of feature development, validate the lever against creator input. A 30-minute interview or short survey with 5–10 lapsing creators could shift prioritization entirely. If creators say "I lapse because I'm too busy and don't know what my audience wants," analytics + scheduling could work. If they say "I lapse because I feel like I'm shouting into the void," engagement prompts or messaging may matter more.

---

Finding 3: Two-Week Silence → Churn Claim Is Stated Assumption, Not Validated Data
Pattern: The brief uses "two-week creator silence → measurable reader churn" as a key behavioral signal to justify focusing on lapsing creators as the highest-impact segment. This claim is positioned as foundational to the theory of success ("removing creator silence breaks the churn cycle"), but no supporting data is provided.  
Evidence: The claim appears in "WHAT WE HAVE: Behavioral signal: Two-week creator silence → measurable reader churn (no recovery mechanism)." No data references, no cohort analysis, no churn curves by creator-posting-frequency are included. The brief also notes under "WHAT WE LACK: Baseline posting frequency and DAU/WAU distribution by segment," suggesting this data has not been pulled.  
Segment Relevance: lapsing-creators (direct; the claim justifies focus on them), reader-retention (indirect; assumes reader churn is the mechanism that matters).  
User Stage Relevance: at_risk_users (lapsing creators; the signal justifies recovery urgency).  
Confidence: Medium — The claim is plausible (industry intuition suggests reader retention decays with creator silence), but untested. It's a reasonable assumption, but if wrong, it could misdirect the entire segment focus.  
Implication: Validate before launch: Pull churn curves by creator-posting-frequency cohort. Measure: What % of readers churn after creator goes silent for 7 days? 14 days? 30 days? Can churned readers be re-engaged if creator resumes posting? This data will refine the recovery window and validate whether 90-day measurement window is realistic.

---

Finding 4: Onboarding Sessions Suggest New-Creator Acquisition Is Currently Higher Priority Than Retention
Pattern: The design session votes show engagement with onboarding and visual design (3 votes across 4 sessions), but zero votes on the proposed feature levers. Additionally, the sessions' problem statements are framed around "maximizing successful community launches" and "reduce drop-off between signup and first value moment"—not around sustaining posting cadence or recovering lapsing creators.  
Evidence: Onboarding sessions: "Guided Tour" has 2 votes, "Template Picker" has 1 vote (both aimed at first-run experience). Dashboard design sessions have 0 votes on both options. Zero discussion of lapse recovery, posting-cadence levers, or creator retention. Session goals cite "80%+ of new users to their first aha moment within 2 minutes" and "maximize successful community launches by July 2026"—acquisition and onboarding success, not lifecycle retention.  
Segment Relevance: regular_users / new_creators (onboarding-focused), not lapsing-creators.  
User Stage Relevance: early-stage users (onboarding sessions), not at_risk_users.  
Confidence: High — Direct observation of session goals and vote distribution.  
Implication: Recommend explicit sequencing decision: Is the Q-level priority new-creator onboarding success or lapsing-creator recovery? Both are valid, but they compete for engineering headcount and design attention. If lapsing-creator recovery is truly highest-ROI (as the brief states), these design sessions may be misaligned. Conversely, if new-creator success is priority, the brief's focus on lapsing creators should be re-framed as a secondary measure or future work.

---

Finding 5: No Measurement of Current Creator Behavior Baseline or Variance by Segment
Pattern: The brief defines target metrics (+25% posting frequency, +15% DAU/WAU, +10% pageviews per session) but provides no baseline distribution by segment. This means we don't know: (a) whether +25% is realistic given current behavior, (b) whether lapsing creators have a different baseline than power users, or (c) how much room there is to move the needle.  
Evidence: "WHAT WE LACK: Baseline posting frequency and DAU/WAU distribution by segment." No data pull for posting_frequency_rolling_7d, posting_frequency_rolling_30d, DAU/WAU by creator_cohort, or churn by posting_frequency_bucket is included in the evidence set.  
Segment Relevance: lapsing-creators (how often do they currently post? what's the ceiling?), high-activity-creators (what posting frequency defines "high-activity"?), creator-teams (do they post differently than solo creators?).  
User Stage Relevance: all stages; baseline variance is essential for segment definition.  
Confidence: High — Explicit gap acknowledged in brief; no counterv

messages.subject: Synthesis: What is the strongest near-term strategy for give creators t
messages.assumptions: Analysis based on available observations and session data.
messages.body:
RESEARCH & INSIGHTS SYNTHESIS
Creator Visibility & Control → Sustained Posting Frequency

---

SUBJECT
Product lever (creator analytics dashboard) should be tested first to drive posting frequency; lifecycle and messaging levers should follow only if analytics shows weak primary signal — but confidence is partial due to absence of creator feedback and usage data.

---

DETAILS
The business hypothesis is sound: creators who see which content works and can schedule ahead will post more consistently, reversing the two-week silence → reader churn cycle. However, we're working with structural evidence only (business context, competitive landscape, metric targets) and zero direct creator input. The design sessions captured in the evidence set are tactical (dashboard styling, onboarding UX) rather than strategic. This synthesis maps the hypothesis to three candidate levers (Product, Lifecycle, Messaging), scores them against the business objective, and recommends a test sequence — but confidence remains partial until we validate that visibility is actually the primary blocker to posting frequency (vs. time, burnout, unclear audience, or other barriers).

---

CONFIDENCE
MEDIUM (leaning toward MEDIUM-LOW pending creator input)

Rationale: We have clear business metrics, segment definitions, and a plausible hypothesis grounded in competitive precedent (Substack, Patreon, Creator.co all ship analytics + scheduling). We do not have:
Creator feedback on actual barriers to posting
Current baseline posting frequency distribution (by segment)
Existing usage data on any partial tooling or lifecycle triggers
Reader churn correlation timing (to prioritize urgency)

The recommendation is directional and testable, but the specific lever ranking will shift once we hear from creators.

---

READINESS
PARTIAL

The business problem is crisp and the hypothesis is testable. However, we lack the foundation data needed to score competing levers with high confidence:
Missing: Creator interviews (3-5 per segment) on barriers to posting frequency
Missing: Usage data on existing scheduler or analytics adoption (if any)
Missing: Baseline posting frequency by segment (to set realistic +25% target)
Missing: Reader churn lag analysis (to sequence intervention timing)

We can recommend a test candidate and measurement plan now; confidence will jump from Medium to High once we collect 2-3 of these signals.

---

TOP FINDINGS
Competitive table-stakes signal (High confidence): Substack, Patreon, and Creator.co all ship creator analytics and scheduling as core features. Raptive Community has neither. This is a credible market signal that visibility + control matter, but does not tell us which lever moves Raptive creators specifically.
Churn velocity is the business forcing function (High confidence): Two weeks of creator silence triggers reader dropout with no recovery mechanism. This defines the intervention window and makes posting frequency (not just DAU) the right primary metric. However, we don't yet know if the barrier to posting is visibility, time, motivation, or something else.
Design session evidence is tactical, not strategic (Medium confidence): The four design sessions in the evidence set focus on dashboard styling, onboarding flow, and wizard structure — not on what creators actually need to post more. Guided Tour (2 votes) vs. Template Picker (1 vote) in the onboarding flow may hint that creators prefer structure + immediate value over blank-slate flexibility, but this is thin and applies to first-run experience, not sustained posting.
Three levers are theoretically distinct but likely interdependent (Medium confidence): Product (analytics/scheduling), Lifecycle (email/in-app triggers), and Messaging (motivational communication) are separable in concept but likely reinforce each other in practice. The design sessions show no explicit prioritization across these levers — they're treated as independent workstreams.
Segment stratification is critical but unvalidated (Medium confidence): High-activity vs. lapsing vs. teams are sensible segments, but we don't know:
What % of creators fall into each segment
Whether lapsing creators are recoverable (vs. permanently churned)
Whether teams have different product/UX needs (e.g., do they need role-based scheduling, approvals, etc.?)

---

TOP NEEDS
Creator barrier interviews (3-5 per segment: high-activity, lapsing, teams) — One-on-one 20-minute calls asking "What's the biggest blocker to posting more often?" and "What would make you post 2-3x more?" This single signal will directionally score each lever (visibility gap → analytics; time constraint → scheduling; motivation gap → messaging). Source: Direct outreach to creator segment; can execute in 1-2 weeks.
Baseline posting frequency distribution + churn lag analysis — Pull current data on posts/creator/week by segment and correlation between creator silence duration and reader session drop-off. This quantifies the recovery window and sets realistic targets for +25% lift. Source: Analytics database or product instrumentation; should exist in current system.
Competitive feature audit: analytics/scheduling UX in Substack, Patreon, Creator.co — Document how competitors surface creator metrics (post performance, audience size, engagement trends) and implement scheduling (batch creation, optimal-time suggestions, recurring posts). This de-risks implementation scope and clarifies table-stakes UX. Source: Competitive deep-dive; can execute in 1 week.

---

RECOMMENDATION

Test the Product lever (creator analytics dashboard) as the primary 90-day test, targeting lapsing creators as the initial segment.

Why this lever, why this segment:
Lever rationale: Visibility is the lowest-friction entry point. It requires no creator behavior change (unlike scheduling, which requires pre-planning); it's a pure information tool. If lapsing creators say "I don't know what worked," analytics directly addresses that gap. Scheduling and messaging are downstream — they're more useful once creators know what works.
Segment rationale: Lapsing creators (dropped to <1 post/week, no posts in 21+ days) are the highest-leverage segment for this business objective. They're revenue-sensitive (reader churn accelerates with silence), but they're not fully churned — they're recoverable with the right signal. High-activity creators are proof-of-concept; teams are a secondary UX problem.
Metric focus: Primary KPI is active_rate (posts/creator/week). Success = +15% (conservative lift) to +25% (target) in lapsing creator posting frequency within 90 days. Secondary: DAU/WAU (proof of habit loop) and pageviews per session (reader consequence).

Hypothesis:
If lapsing creators can see post-level performance data (views, engagement, reader comments, read time) within a clean analytics dashboard, then they will return to posting 1-2x per week (vs. current <1 post/week baseline), because visibility restores motivation and clarifies what works.

Test design sketch:
Sample: Lapsing creators only (n=300-500, cohort defined as: posted 2+ times/week for 8+ weeks, then <1 post/week for 21+ days, no posts in last 14 days)
Control: Random 50% holdout (no analytics dashboard); treatment 50% (full analytics dashboard shipped)
Dashboard scope (MVP): Post-level metrics (views, engagement, top performer), audience size trend, time-series of posting frequency, one clear CTA ("Schedule next post")
Measurement window: 90 days from first dashboard view
Go/no-go: If lapsing creators in treatment group post +15% more than control group within 60 days, green-light Scaling phase (add scheduling, expand to high-activity and teams). If <+10%, investigate failure modes (is analytics working? is visibility not the blocker?) and pivot to Lifecycle lever.

---

PHASE
Learning phase

Rationale: We have a clear hypothesis but no product validation yet. This test is designed to learn which lever moves active_rate most before we commit to Scaling investment. Once we confirm analytics (or another lever) moves the needle 15%+, we transition to Scaling — shipping to broader cohorts, adding complementary features (scheduling, lifecycle triggers), and optimizing for +25% target.

---

ASSUMPTIONS
Creator posting motivation is primarily constrained by visibility/clarity, not time or burnout. If creators are posting less because they're burned out, don't know who their audience is, or feel the platform doesn't work, analytics alone won't help. We'll test this assumption directly.
Lapsing creators are recoverable within 90 days with a single product intervention. If they've churned deeper (platform distrust, switched to competitor, lost audience), analytics won't bring them back. This is a segment risk.
The two-week silence → reader churn lag is consistent and measurable. If churn happens before two weeks or is delayed by weeks, the intervention window and urgency shift.
Scheduling and messaging are downstream of visibility. Once creators see what works, they'll be more willing to plan ahead and engage with motivational messaging. If scheduling and messaging are independent blockers (e.g., creators know what works but don't have time to batch-create), then our lever sequencing is wrong.
Pageviews per session will lift when creator posting frequency lifts. Assumes reader behavior is responsive to more content and not cannibalizing or compressing.
The 90-day window is sufficient to detect signal and is not confounded by seasonality, external campaigns, or platform changes. We'll need to control for these in the test design.

---

FINDINGS

Finding 1: Competitive Evidence Points to Visibility + Scheduling as Table-Stakes
Pattern: Substack, Patreon, and Creator.co all ship creator analytics and scheduling as core features. This market signal suggests visibility and control are expected by creators.

Evidence: Implicit in business brief context ("Competitors (Substack, Patreon, Creator.co) have creator analytics and scheduling as table stakes").

Segment relevance: All three: high-activity, lapsing, teams. Market sets floor for feature expectations.

User stage relevance: Power users (who may have used competitors) and at-risk users (who are comparing alternatives).

Confidence: High. This is not user feedback; it's market structure. The question is not whether analytics + scheduling matter broadly, but whether they matter for Raptive creators specifically and in what order.

---

Finding 2: Two-Week Silence → Reader Churn Correlation Defines Intervention Urgency
Pattern: The brief states that "Two weeks of creator silence triggers reader churn with no recovery mechanism." This sets a hard deadline for intervention and makes posting frequency (not just DAU) the right primary metric.

Evidence: Explicit in business brief ("Business Problem: Two weeks of creator silence triggers reader churn with no recovery mechanism").

Segment relevance: Lapsing creators (primary) and high-activity creators who may face temporary silence. Not relevant to teams unless they have different silence dynamics.

User stage relevance: At-risk users (early signals of reduced posting). This is the cohort where intervention has highest leverage.

Confidence: Medium. The brief states the pattern as given; we don't yet have the lag/correlation data to confirm timing or magnitude. This should be validated in the 90-day test.

---

Finding 3: Design Sessions Indicate Preference for Structure + Immediate Value in Onboarding
Pattern: In the "Onboarding Flow" design session, Guided Tour (2 votes) vs. Template Picker (1 vote) suggests creators prefer interactive structure and templates over blank-slate flexibility. Progressive Disclosure (0 votes) scored lowest.

Evidence: Design session voting on "Onboarding Flow" — Guided Tour 2 votes, Template Picker 1 vote, Progressive Disclosure 0 votes. Total votes: 3 across entire evidence set.

Segment relevance: Regular users and new creators (onboarding cohort). Less relevant to high-activity and lapsing creators.

User stage relevance: New users entering onboarding. May indicate that at_risk_users (existing creators considering abandonment) are not represented in this design session voting.

Confidence: Low. Only 3 votes total across the onboarding session, and no context on who voted (are these creators or internal stakeholders?). This is a tactical onboarding UX signal, not a strategic insight into what drives posting frequency.

---

Finding 4: Dashboard Modernization Is Table-Stakes for Creator Confidence but Not a Posting Frequency Driver
Pattern: Design sessions on "Dashboard Widget Style" (glassmorphism vs. flat minimal) indicate platform concern about dashboard dated-ness and visual hierarchy. Creators report "hard to spot trends at a glance." However, the goal is design satisfaction (4+ on 5-point scale), not posting frequency.

Evidence: Design session problem statement: "Current dashboard looks dated — plain tables with no visual hierarchy. Users say it's hard to spot trends at a glance." Goal: "Ship a refreshed dashboard that scores 4+ on a 5-point design satisfaction survey."

Segment relevance: Potentially all segments, but especially power users (who spend more time in analytics) and at-risk users (if poor visibility demotivates posting).

User stage relevance: All stages, but power users are most vocal about UX debt.

Confidence: Medium. We know dashboard aesthetics matter for user satisfaction, but we don't know if a redesign alone will lift posting frequency. This is hygiene work, not the lever.

---

Finding 5: Product, Lifecycle, and Messaging Levers Are Theoretically Distinct but Not Explicitly Prioritized in Current Design Queue
Pattern: The four design sessions span onboarding (process), dashboard styling (UX), and onboarding strategy (content/guidance). None explicitly address analytics, scheduling, lifecycle triggers (email/notifications), or motivational messaging. This suggests the three candidate levers have not yet been scoped or prioritized as competing alternatives.

Evidence: Design sessions focus on tactical UI/UX (widget styling, onboarding flow) and onboarding strategy (Community Onboarding session), not on strategic lever choices (Product vs. Lifecycle vs. Messaging).

Segment relevance: Suggests a Product/Lifecycle/Messaging decision has not been made yet — all three remain open as possibilities.

User stage relevance: Applies to all stages, as the lever choice cascades through the product.

Confidence: High. The absence of explicit lever prioritization in the design sessions is a directional finding in itself.

---

OBJECTIVE MAPPING

Business Objective: "Give creators the visibility and control they need to show up consistently — so that community becomes a place they're invested in, not just present on."

| Lever | How It Maps | Evidence | Confidence |
|-------|------------|----------|------------|
| Product (Analytics Dashboard) | Visibility: creators see post-level performance, audience trends, top content. Control: scheduling (future). Directly addresses "invested" vs. "present" — creators who know what works post more. | Competitive precedent (Substack, Patreon ship analytics). Churn correlation implies visibility is missing. Design sessions show dashboard is dated (hygiene work, but necessary). | Medium |
| Lifecycle (Email/In-App Triggers) | Timing: re-engagement emails at moments when creators are most likely to return ("You have a new reader comment!" or "Your post is trending"). Drives "consistency" through habit prompts. | No direct evidence in data set. Implicit in brief's "lifecycle" mention. Could be powerful if combined with analytics (e.g., "Post X got 100 views, here's what worked"). | Low-Medium |
| Messaging (Motivational Communication) | Perception: clarify value of posting ("Your community is growing," "Readers care about your voice"). Addresses burnout and motivation gaps if visibility alone isn't enough. | No direct evidence. Could be table-stakes or orthogonal depending on whether creator motivation gap exists. | Low |

Metric Alignment:
active_rate (posting frequency, +25% target): Product lever (analytics) has strongest direct path — creators see what works, post more. Lifecycle reinforces through timing. Messaging enables if motivation is the blocker.
DAU/WAU ratio (+15%): All three levers contribute — analytics drives return visits; lifecycle triggers sessions; messaging frames value.
Pageviews per session (+10%): Consequence of posting frequency lift; all levers upstream.

Recommendation: Prioritize Product (analytics) → then Lifecycle (triggers) → then Messaging (motivation), based on plausibility and execution risk. Product is least dependent on creator motivation (it's a pure information tool); Lifecycle and Messaging are downstream once we know creators actually use analytics.

---

ADDITIONAL SIGNALS WORTH GATHERING

Signal 1: Creator Barrier Interviews (3-5 per segment)
What: 20-minute calls with high-activity, lapsing, and team creators asking: "What's the biggest blocker to posting more often?" and "What would make you post 2-3x more?"

Why it matters: Will directionally score each lever. If lapsing creators say "I don't know what worked," Product (analytics) is hot. If they say "I don't have time," Lifecycle/Scheduling is hot. If they say "I'm burned out,"
messages.nextStep: Review findings and consider prototyping recommendations.
